text: The surgeon closes the anterior defect using a permanent suture, ensuring that some suture bites incorporate portions of the mesh left in place.
The surgeon begins suturing at the inferior aspect of the hernia defect, ensuring a reasonable overlap of the mesh behind or below the defect.

That was a twelve inch suture. You can see already that I've used up the entire stitch almost. And I used several of these during the case. We only had twelve inch sutures available. I think eighteen might have been a little bit more appropriate.
The surgeon imbricates the hernia sac to reduce dead space, folding and suturing the tissue to minimize the cavity left by the hernia.

The surgeon parks the needle temporarily and begins suturing from the top, starting just below the xiphoid process. The tension on the hernia defect closure is visually assessed, with the pressure reduced to eight during the process.

The surgeon starts suturing at the top and bottom of the hernia defect, with the assistant pushing down on the hernia sac to facilitate catching suture bites. The surgeon sequentially tightens the sutures with each throw to decrease tension.

The surgeon places a new stitch in the center of the hernia defect, cinching it to bring the tissue together and reduce tension. The stitch visibly helps align the defect as the surgeon continues to suture from the top and bottom.

The surgeon continues suturing from the top, progressively tightening the stitches to close the hernia defect. As the sutures are tightened, the rectus muscles visibly move towards the midline, indicating successful restoration of anatomical alignment.
The surgeon continues to use each suture until its end, progressively closing the defect as the tension is relieved.
I always think it's nice to see when you cut your suture that the end that you just cut doesn't retract back into the muscle to make you feel like maybe there was too much tension.

The surgeon sutures the linear defect together, incorporating bits of mesh, ensuring the hernia sac is imbricated and touches the top of the rectus muscles anteriorly. The permanent suture is used to maintain closure.
The surgeon finishes placing an additional suture at the bottom of the hernia repair site. A visible gap is addressed by starting in the middle and running another suture to close it.

But overall, I was really pleased with how well this defect came together. I don't do these surgeries as frequently as a lot of the other experts. As I like to say, I'm just a colon surgeon. And so big defects like this don't come my way that often. But I was very pleased with how that came together.

The surgeon demonstrates the closure of multiple holes on the left superior side of the posterior sheath, showing how the defects have been successfully brought together using a running 3-0 B-lock suture.

The surgeon observes the redundancy in the tissue after the transversus abdominis release (TAR), noting the absence of tension as the posterior elements are closed, ensuring a tension-free midline closure.

And I think it's important to close these holes for anyone who doesn't do these operations that frequently for two reasons. One is to not have any uncoated mesh exposed to the viscera. And two, and maybe even more importantly, is the risk of a hernia post-op, of a loop of bowel sneaking in there. We've certainly seen videos like that before. um using a little bit of the extra of that suture to close a defect up here now this was above the liver i guess you could argue not necessary to close but i was uncomfortable leaving it open and i had the suture so i just went ahead and closed it

The surgeon identifies another defect in the left upper quadrant and proceeds to close it, demonstrating the closure of all trocar-related holes along the left-hand side. The surgical instruments are redocked on the opposite side to facilitate closure.
The surgeon closes one of the visible defects on the patient's right side using sutures, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.

This does take time. Obviously, the best method to decrease that amount of time is not to create the injuries in the first place. It does invariably happen. I have realized that over my time of doing these cases, I make less and less holes.

The surgeon closes the posterior elements starting at the top, with the camera docked on the patient's left side. The view is directed towards the xiphoid, where the diaphragm is visible. The posterior opening is closed down the midline, with the tissue coming together smoothly and without tension, lying flat against the viscera.
The surgeon performs a transversus abdominis release, extending the dissection laterally to ensure the closure comes together without tension.

The surgeon measures the hernia defect, noting dimensions of approximately forty centimeters longitudinally and thirty-three to thirty-four centimeters horizontally. The left upper quadrant port is upsized to twelve millimeters to accommodate the insertion of a Versatex mesh, which is cut to dimensions of thirty-eight to thirty-nine by thirty-three centimeters.
The surgeon unfolds the mesh laparoscopically, maneuvering the camera to view both sides of the abdomen as needed during the process.

The surgeon laparoscopically unfolds the mesh, adjusting the camera through a different port to view the right upper quadrant. The mesh is carefully unrolled to ensure it lays completely flat without using fixation methods such as transfacials or glue.
The surgeon observes the mesh placement in the retrorectus space, noting its flat appearance. Drains are left in place following the dissection, and the surgical field is desufflated.

The surgeon closes the left upper quadrant 12mm port site using a suture passer. A drain is positioned emerging from the left lower quadrant port, placed centrally. The surgical field is then desufflated.
